coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot for direct fluorescence detection of Al3+ in aqueous media and living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Bi-Yun
Li, Cheng
Song, Yuan-Yang
Tan, Fang
Cao, Yuan-Cheng
Zhao, Yuan-Di
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dot (GQD) has been attractive in analytical science field due to its low toxicity, stable photoluminescence. Herein, nitrogen-doped GQD (N-GQD) was prepared by a facile solvothermal treatment of GO using dimethylformamide, and exhibited a green emission with 23.1% quantum yield. The N-GQD probe showed a selective and sensitive fluorescence enhancement response to Al3+, the mechanism might be the formation of a complex between Al3+ and N-GQD constrained the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process of N-GQD itself. With Benesi-Hildebrand equation, the binding constant and molar ratio between N-GQD and Al3+ was calculated to be 4.6 × 104 Lmol−1 and 1:1 respectively. The pK a value of N-GQD was also determined to be 4.4 by capillary electrophoresis. In pH 4.0 PBS solution, there was a good linear relation between the fluorescence intensity and the logarithm of concentration of Al3+ in the range of 2.5–75μmolL−1, the limit of detection (3σ) was 1.3μmolL−1. This “Off - On” fluorescence method had been applied to accurate quantification of aluminum in hydrotalcite tablets. What's more, the fluorescence switch property of N-GQD was explored by alternate addition of Al3+ and EDTA. The probe was also utilized for detection Al3+ in living cells due to its excellent biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot
Fluorescence enhancing
Al3+ detection
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Binding constant and binding ratio between N-GQD and Al3+
HeLa cells fluorescence imaging and detection
pK a value of N-GQD and effect of pH on N-GQD fluorescence response to Al3+
The fluorescence intensity of GQD and GQD + Al3+ change with time
Detection of Al3+ in hydrotalcite tablets
Detection of Al3+ in HeLa cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQD. (A) AFM image of N-GQD. (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-GQD aqueous solution. Inset was the picture of N-GQD under daylight and UV-light. (C) FT-IR spectra of GO (a), N-GQD (b), N-GQD-Al3+ (c), and Al(NO3)3 (d).

Fluorescence enhancement mechanism. (A) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQD without (a) and with (b) 50μmolL−1 Al3+. (B) UV/vis absorption spectra of 10μmolL−1Al3+ (a) and N-GQD + Al3+ (b-d), from curve b-d, the concentrations of Al3+ were 0, 1, and 10μmolL−1Al3+ respectively, the inset showed the amplifying diagram of the absorption spectra.

pK a value determination of N-GQD by capillary electrophoresis. (A) Electropherogram of the mixing solution of N-GQD and rhodamine B at pH 7 (a, b) and pH 5.5 (c, d), a and c were collected at channel of 580nm, b and d were recorded at channel of 550nm. (B) Electropherogram obtained from channel of 580nm at different pH electrolytes, from curve a to g, pH were 7.0, 6.5, 6.0, 5.5, 5.0, 4.5, and 4.3, respectively. (C) Electrophoretic mobility of N-GQD as a function of pH. (D) Reciprocal of mobilities versus the activity of the H+. λex = 420nm.

Fluorescence responses of N-GQD to different concentrations of Al3+ in PBS (pH 4.0). (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQD in the presence of increasing concentrations of Al3+ (the concentrations from curve a to m were 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100μmolL−1, respectively). (B) The relationship between fluorescence intensity at 475nm and concentration of Al3+. (C) Calibration curve of Al3+ detection. (D) Linear fitting curve based on Benesi-Hildebrand equation, F
0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of N-GQD and N-GQD + Al3+ respectively.
Fluorescence intensity changes at 475nm of N-GQD upon alternate addition of different concentrations of Al3+ (50, 60, 70 and 80μmolL−1) and 80μmolL−1 EDTA in PBS (pH 4.0).
Confocal images of HeLa cells incubated with N-GQD for different time (A); Confocal images for detection Al3+ in HeLa cells (B). The scale of images was 20µm.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot for direct fluorescence detection of Al3+ in aqueous media and living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Britton Chance Center for Biomedical Photonics at Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics – Hubei Bioinformatics & Molecular Imaging Key Laboratory, Key Laboratory of Biomedical Photonics (HUST, Ministry of Education), Collaborative Innovation Center for Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Science and Technology, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Britton Chance Center for Biomedical Photonics at Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics – Hubei Bioinformatics & Molecular Imaging Key Laboratory, Key Laboratory of Biomedical Photonics (HUST, Ministry of Education), Collaborative Innovation Center for Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Science and Technology, Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices, Ministry of Education, Jianghan University, Wuhan 430056, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices, Ministry of Education, Jianghan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dot (GQD) has been attractive in analytical science field due to its low toxicity, stable photoluminescence. Herein, nitrogen-doped GQD (N-GQD) was prepared by a facile solvothermal treatment of GO using dimethylformamide, and exhibited a green emission with 23.1% quantum yield. The N-GQD probe showed a selective and sensitive fluorescence enhancement response to Al3+, the mechanism might be the formation of a complex between Al3+ and N-GQD constrained the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process of N-GQD itself. With Benesi-Hildebrand equation, the binding constant and molar ratio between N-GQD and Al3+ was calculated to be 4.6 × 104 Lmol−1 and 1:1 respectively. The pK a value of N-GQD was also determined to be 4.4 by capillary electrophoresis. In pH 4.0 PBS solution, there was a good linear relation between the fluorescence intensity and the logarithm of concentration of Al3+ in the range of 2.5–75μmolL−1, the limit of detection (3σ) was 1.3μmolL−1. This “Off - On” fluorescence method had been applied to accurate quantification of aluminum in hydrotalcite tablets. What's more, the fluorescence switch property of N-GQD was explored by alternate addition of Al3+ and EDTA. The probe was also utilized for detection Al3+ in living cells due to its excellent biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:para: The graphene sheets with lateral dimensions less than one hundred nanometers are called graphene quantum dots (GQD). Synthesis of GQD can be classified into “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods. The former is prepared by cleaving carbonaceous materials through chemical or physical methods (Dong et al., 2012). The latter is realized by step-wise chemical fusion of small organic molecules with heat energy (Hsu and Chang, 2012). GQD emerges new chemical and physical properties, such as considerably low toxicity, high stability, excellent solubility, photoluminescence (PL), and good biocompatibility. Among them, PL of a GQD is essentially originated from the quantum confinement and edge effects (Zheng et al., 2015). As it was reported, GQD could be served as fluorescent sensor for biological and chemical substances by directly synthesis of functional GQD or modification of GQD with functional molecule. For example, Jiang et al. (2015) prepared nitrogen-doped GQD (N-GQD) via rapid microwave irradiation of glycerol and ethylenediamine for Pb2+ detection through fluorescence quenching method; Zhou et al. (2014) synthesized molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP)-coated GQD for selectively fluorescent determination of paranitrophenol; Qu's group designed an assay for thrombin detection with aptamer-functionalized carbon nanodot (C-dot) (Xu et al., 2012).

Aluminum (Al) metal and its compounds exist in environment and human daily life, such as aluminum alloy has been used as cookware, the compounds of aluminum also can serve as clinical drugs (Liu Y. et al., 2017). However, the excessive intake of Al is harmful to liver, kidney, and bones of human body, especially can induce Alzheimer's disease and osteoporosis (Perl et al., 1982). Furthermore, the acid rain caused by environment pollution enhances the transformation of compounds of Al to Al3+, the latter one is easily soluble in soil and adsorbed by plant, which is a major factor limiting plant production (Delhaize et al., 1995). Therefore, the development of a facile, sensitive and selective method for Al3+ detection is essential to monitoring environment and maintaining human healthy.

At present, the main methods for Al3+ detection were atomic absorption or emission spectrometry and inductively coupled plasma massspectrometry (ICP-MS) (Frankowski and Ziola-Frankowska, 2010; Labrecque and Lariviere, 2014). Although providing accurate results, these techniques involved complex operation and expensive instrumentation. Fluorescence spectrophotometry which possessed merits in high sensitivity and simplicity was generally adopted. Up to now, most fluorescent probes for Al3+ were based on organic chemosensors (Maity and Govindaraju, 2010; Diao et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2017), only a few were based on fluorescent carbon dots (C-dot). For instance, Kim et al. (2015) designed a rhodamine-appended C-dot for sensing Al3+ through ratiometric fluorescence method, the mechanism was the chelation of rhodamine with Al3+ induced Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from C-dot to rhodamine; Yan et al. (2016) synthesized a C-dot from citric acid, the fluorescence of C-dot was significantly quenched by dopamine, while could be recovered by Al3+ on account of high affinity between dopamine and Al3+, thus realized Al3+ detection through indirect method; similarly, a C-dot was prepared from citric acid and diethylenetriamine by Zou et al. (2014), the fluorescence of the C-dot could be efficiently quenched by quercetin, the complexation of quercetin and Al3+ leaded FRET from C-dot to the complex, this ratiometric fluorescence strategy was constructed for testing Al3+. All these methods for Al3+ detection were based on further functional modified C-dots through indirect strategies.

Here, a nitrogen-doped graphene quantum (N-GQD) was prepared by facile solvothermal treatment with GO using dimethylformamide, and it showed direct and selective fluorescence enhance response towards Al3+. The sensitive and fast fluorescence enhance method for Al3+ detection based on N-GQD avoided interference factors existing in indirect quantification methods. Meanwhile, the pK a value of N-GQD and the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement of N-GQD caused by Al3+ was also investigated, and this sensor was applied to the detect Al3+ in practical sample. What's more, the fluorescence switch property of N-GQD regulated by Al3+ and EDTA was researched. According to the report, the N-GQD with good biocompatibility could enter cells by endocytosis (Zhu et al., 2011), thus we firstly used it for detecting Al3+ in living cells by confocal fluorescence imaging.

The main instruments used in experiments including LGJ-10C freeze drier (Four-Ring Science Instrument Plant Beijing Co., Ltd, CHN), KSY-5-12A electric furnace (Tianjin Zhonghuan Lab Furnace Co., Ltd, CHN). SPM-9700 atomic force microscope (Shimadzu, JPN), VERTEX 70 fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Bruker, GER), and ZS90 Zetasizer Nano (Malvern, UK), and AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600W X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (Shimadzu, JPN) were used for characterization of N-GQD.

The content of Al3+ in tablets for contrast was measured by iCAP Q mass spectrometer with inductively coupled plasma (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis spectra were measured by LS-55 florescence spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) and UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, JPN), respectively. HeLa cells were imaged by FV 1000 laser-scanning confocal microscopy (Shimadzu, JPN). Ultrapure water (≥ 18.2MΩ • cm) was obtained from Rephile system (CHN). Buffer solutions at different pH were regulated by FE20 pH meter (Mettler Toledo, SUI).

CdCl2·2.5H2O and NiSO4·6H2O were purchased from Shanghai Ling Feng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (CHN). Pb(NO3)2 was obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (USA). CoCl2·6H2O was purchased from Sigma Aldrich Fine Chemicals (USA). N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA), Al(NO3)3·9H2O, SrCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, BaCl2·2H2O, SnCl2·2H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, CaCl2, Hg(NO3)2·0.5H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, NaCl and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (CHN). The solutions of Al3+ and other metal ions were prepared by the relevant metallic compounds. Hydrotalcite tablets were produced by Sichuan Jewelland Pharamaceutical Co., Ltd (CHN).
Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) of different pH values were prepared by titrating 0.01molL−1 phosphoric acid solution with a concentrated sodium hydroxide solution (1molL−1).

GO was synthesized from graphitic power according to modified Hummer's method (Dong et al., 2010). Briefly, graphite powder (0.75g) was added into a solution consisting of concentrated H2SO4 (4.5mL), K2S2O8 (1.125g) and P2O5 (1.125g) and reacted at 80°C for 5h. After cooling to room temperature, deionized water (100mL) was slowly added. The product was filtered by 0.2µm Nylon film and dried naturally. Pretreated graphite powder was further oxidized by 20mL H2O2 (30%) and KMnO4 (10g) at 38°C for 5h. After that, the reaction was ended by addition of deionized water (200mL), and then 20mL H2O2 (30%). At the end, the mixture was repeated washed with 1: 10 (v: v) HCl aqueous solution, and then deionized water. Finally, graphene oxide (2mgmL−1) was exfoliated by sonicating for 2h under ice bath. The GO solid was obtained by removing aqueous medium with vacuum freeze drier.

250mg of freeze-dried GO was dispersed in 15mL of DMF. After ultrasonic for 30min (200W), the mixture was transferred into poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave and heated at 200°C for 5h. After cooling to room temperature, the black precipitate was rejected after centrifugation. The supernate with N-GQD was further filtered by 0.2µm Nylon film filter. The N-GQD solid was obtained by evaporating the solvents with a vacuum rotary evaporator. Finally, brown solution of N-GQD was got with addition of 3mL deionized water and preserved in 4°C, which could retain stability for at least six months.

The Q of N-GQD was calculated by quinine sulfate solution (QR = 0.546, 0.05molL−1 H2SO4) as reference together with the following formula (Zhu et al., 2011):
Q
=
Q
R
×
[
I
×
A
R
×
η
2
]
/
[
I
R
×
A
×
η
R
2
]
η is the refractive index of the solvent, A is the absorbance at excitation wavelength (the absorbance is kept in 0.02–0.05) and I is the integrated fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding constant and binding ratio between N-GQD and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para:
The binding constant (K) and binding ratio (n) between N-GQD and Al3+ were calculated based on the Benesi-Hildebrand equation (Wang et al., 2017):
1
F −
F
0
=
1
K (
F
1
−
F
0
)
[ M ]
n
+
1
F
1
−
F
0

F
0 , F, and F
1 are the emission intensities at 475nm of N-GQD considered in the absence of Al3+, at an intermediate Al3+ concentration, and at a concentration of complete interaction, respectively; [M] is the concentration of Al3+.

In 5mL colorimetric tubes, successively added 8μL N-GQD (5mgmL−1), 1mL PBS (pH 4.0，0.01molL−1), and 5μL different concentrations of Al3+ or sample solution, then, adjusted the volumes to 2.5mL scales. The mixed solutions were equilibrated for 15min before spectral measurements. The excitation wavelength of fluorescence spectrophotometer was set as 370nm, excitation and emission slit widths were both 10.

Five tablets were crushed to powder. About 0.2g weighted powder was dissolved by 3mL HCl (6molL−1) and heated with water bath for 15min. After cooling, the sample solution was adjusted to neutral by NaOH (2molL−1), then transferred to a 25mL volumetric flask and diluted to the scale with water. The final sample solution was obtained by filtering with 0.2µm film and preserved in 4°C. The sample had to be diluted into an appropriate range of concentration with deionized water before testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HeLa cells fluorescence imaging and detection
ce:section.ce:para: The HeLa cells dispersed in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin–streptomycin were seeded on five confocal dishes, and incubated under an humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air at 37°C for 15h. In the experiment of cell uptake kinetics, the probe N-GQD (100μgmL−1) was added into different culture dishes at intervals. After incubation with N-GQD for different time, the HeLa cells were washed with PBS (pH 7.4, 0.01molL−1) and kept in PBS (1mL) for confocal fluorescence imaging.

For detection of Al3+ in HeLa cells, after the seeded cells grew for 15h, the culture medium in each dish was replaced by new 1mL DMEM containing the 100μgmL−1 N-GQD. All dishes were incubated in an incubator for 2h for N-GQD uptake. After that, the excess N-GQD was removed by washing three times with PBS. Then, 1mL DMEM containing different concentrations of Al3+ (0, 10, 20, 30 and 50μmolL−1) were added into different dishes respectively. After further incubation for 2h, the HeLa cells were washed three times with PBS and kept in PBS (1mL) for imaging on confocal fluorescence microscopy with excitation at 405nm and emission at 520nm ± 10nm using an oil objective lens (60×).

Following, the above treated cells and the control cells (without N-GQD) were collected by digestion with trypsin and kept in PBS (1mL). After counting the number of the cells, six cell groups were broken by ultrasonic wave and centrifuged at 13,700g for 10min. Then, the supernatant of cells were measured by florescence spectrophotometer.

Atomic force microscope (AFM) image of N-GQD ( Fig. 1A) showed their average height was 2.3nm, the size was about 20–30nm. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-GQD (Fig. 1B) showed the maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 370nm and 515nm, respectively. The insert picture in Fig. 1B displayed N-GQD emitted bright green color light under the irradiation of UV-ligth (8W). The compositions of the produced N-GQD were characterized by XPS. Fig. S1 showed survey spectra of N-GQD (Supplementary material), there were three predominant peaks at 285.0, 400.1 and 530.1eV, corresponding to the C1S, N1S and O1S, respectively. The concentrations of nitrogen and oxygen were 12.4% and 33.6%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS of C1S (Fig. S1B, Supplementary material) indicated that there were three kinds of carbons. The peaks at 285.0, 286.2, and 288.6eV were assigned to C˭C, C-OH, and carbonyl C or C-N, respectively. The two peaks at 400.1 and 401.9eV in the N1S spectrum of N-GQD (Fig. S1C, Supplementary material) were assigned to C-N-C and N-(C)3 bonds, respectively. The XPS analyses clearly demonstrated that nitrogen atoms had been successfully doped into the N-GQD. FT-IR spectra showed the stretching vibration bands of C˭C (1631cm−1), C˭O (1728cm−1) and O-H (3440cm−1) were all existed for both GO and N-GQD (Fig. 1C, curve a and b). Compared with the spectrum of GO, band at 1073cm−1 (stretching vibration C-O-C) disappeared, while two new bands at 1121cm−1 (stretching vibration C-N-C) and 2780cm−1 (stretching vibration C-H) could be observed in the spectrum of N-GQD. It could be inferred that nitrogen-doping mainly occurred on the plane of GO by opening epoxy group and introducing the dimethylamido from DMF during the solvothermal treatment. Zeta potential values from −30.1mV of GO changed to −8.1mV of N-GQD, the decrease of particles electronegativity also confirmed nitrogen-doping for N-GQD.

The photoluminescence property of N-GQD arose from quantum confinement effects (Zheng et al., 2015). As the fluorescence spectra ( Fig. 2A) showed, the maximum emission peak of N-GQD appeared at 515nm, while blue-shifted to 470nm with an remarkable fluorescence enhancement upon addition of 50μmolL−1Al3+. The wavelength shift indicated the structure of N-GQD might be changed in the presence of Al3+, which could be the nitrogen on dimethylamido and the oxygen on carboxyl of N-GQD strongly combined with Al3+. Fig. 2B exhibited the UV–vis absorption spectra. Al3+ showed typical absorption below 240nm (curve a). The N-GQD also showed strong absorption under 450nm (curve b), while with increasing concentrations of Al3+ (curve b-d), the intensity of absorption of the N-GQD above 268nm gradually decreased and the new absorption bellow 268nm dramatically increased, indicating a new complex with a strong absorption bellow 268nm was formed and decreasing the amounts of free N-GQD. Thus, the isosbestic point at 268nm also illustrated N-GQD could form a complex with Al3+ (Zhang et al., 2010). The combination between N-GQD and Al3+ also characterized through FT-IR (Fig. 1C, curve c). The sample was produced from drying N-GQD and Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution under 60°C. According to the spectrum of Al(NO3)3 (Fig. 1C, curve d), the bands at 1655cm−1 and 1384cm−1 in spectrum of N-GQD-Al3+ were assigned to crystal water and stretching vibration of NO3 -. Compared with N-GQD, the stretching vibration of C-N-C of N-GQD-Al3+ was divided into two bands at 1240cm−1 and 1101cm−1, which was a result of the coordination of the nitrogen atoms in N-GQD with Al3+.

It could be inferred the fluorescence enhancement mechanism was attributed to the restriction of photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process (Liu T. et al., 2017) of N-GQD itself by Al3+. The lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom could be freely transferred to the empty electronic orbit of excited GQD, thus preventing excited state electron directly back to ground state and photoluminescence. However, the above-mentioned PET process could be totally restrained when the electron donating ability of the nitrogen atom was reduced by complexation with Al3+. As a result, a dramatically increased fluorescence emission of N-GQD was observed with addition of Al3+.

The binding constant and binding ratio between N-GQD and Al3+ were calculated based on Benesi-Hildebrand equation (Fig. 4D). Through the linear fitting, a good linearity with correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.991 between 1/[F-F
0 ] and 1/[M] only existed when n = 1, indicating the stoichiometric ratio between N-GQD and Al3+ was 1: 1. The binding constant evaluated from the slop of the linear fitting equation was 4.6 × 104 Lmol−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pK a value of N-GQD and effect of pH on N-GQD fluorescence response to Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Determination of pK a value of N-GQD could help to understand the pH dependent fluorescence enhancing efficiency of N-GQD induced by Al3+. The pK a value of N-GQD was obtained by capillary electrophoresis (CE) with fluorescence detection (Gluck et al., 1996; Perez-Urquiza and Beltran, 2001), which was based on that a solute had its maximum electrophoretic mobility when it was fully ionized, had the lowest mobility in its protonated form, and had an intermediate mobility in the pH region surrounding its pK a. The experiments were conducted through detecting the N-GQD solutions which containing rhodamine B as the electroosmotic flow marker (Li et al., 2009) by CE under different pH electrolyte buffers. As displayed in Fig. 3A, the CE spectra of the samples at pH 7.0 (curve a and b) and pH 5.5 (curve c and d) electrolyte buffers were recorded at two wavelength channels of 580nm (black curve) and 550nm (red curve), respectively. Much stronger fluorescence signals of peak 1 collected at 580nm than at 550nm indicated the peak 1 was generated by rhodamine B, whose maximum wavelength is 580nm. Owing to the excitation-dependent PL behavior of N-GQD (Zhu et al., 2011), a band fluorescence peak centered at 550nm appeared when the N-GQD was excited at wavelength of 420nm. Thus the fluorescence intensity of peak 2 which represented N-GQD had no difference at emission wavelength of 550 and 580nm, respectively. Therefore, to observe the rhodamine B and N-GQD clearly, electrophoretic data of the samples at different pH were obtained from the channel of 580nm. Fig. 3B showed the samples detected in buffers in the range of pH 7.0–4.3, the blue signals and red signals were assigned to rhodamine B and N-GQD, respectively. The CE peak of N-GQD was single under pH 7.0 (curve a), while split into two peaks under electrophoretic buffer of pH 6.5-4.5 (curve b-f), proving N-GQD was fully ionized at pH 7.0, ionized partially at 4.5–6.5. The mobility of N-GQD (μ) was calculated based on Eq. (3) (Supplementary material), the μ of which at pH 4.5–6.5 were calculated from its split peaks with short migration times. As it was shown in Fig. 3C, the absolute values of mobility of N-GQD gradually increased with the increasing pH, because more negative charged N-GQD moved more quickly against the direction of electric field. A good linear relation (R2 = 0.979) existed between 1/μ and the measured concentration of H+ in the range of 1–50.1μmolL−1 (Fig. 3D). According to slop and intercept of the equation (Eq. (1) in Supplementary material), the pK a of N-GQD was calculated to be 4.4.

pH value was a key factor for this fluorescence enhancement system. As shown in results, in the range of pH 3.0–10.0, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQD nearly did not changed (Fig. S2, a, Supplementary material), while the increased fluorescence of N-GQD by Al3+ was greatly affected (Fig. S2, b, Supplementary material). There was the highest enhancing efficiency at pH 4.0. According to the pK a value, the nitrogen on the surface of N-GQD protonated at more acidic condition (pH less than 4), thus resulted the decreasing of the fluorescence enhancing efficiency. In neutral or alkaline solutions, nearly no enhancement effect emerged, which might be resulted from the partial hydrolysis of Al3+ decreased the amount of free Al3+. Therefore, the buffer solution at pH 4.0 was chosen for following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence intensity of GQD and GQD + Al3+ change with time
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of time on the fluorescence of N-GQD in the absence and presence of Al3+ were investigated. The results showed N-GQD possessed good photostability (Fig. S3, a, Supplementary material). After addition of Al3+, fluorescence of system increased rapidly and reached the maximum value at 4min, and kept constant after that (Fig. S3, b, Supplementary material), illustrating a quickly reaction equilibrium existed between N-GQD and Al3+, and the formed complex had stable fluorescence signal. In experiments, fluorescence measurements were performed after reaction equilibrium for 15min.

The fluorescence spectra ( Fig. 4A) exhibited that the fluorescence intensity of N-GQD gradually increased and maximum emission wavelength also gradually blue-shifted with increasing concentrations of Al3+. A good linear relation (inset in Fig. 4C) existed between the fluorescence intensity at 475nm and the logarithm of concentration of Al3+ in the range of 2.5–75μmolL−1 with the linear regression equation of F = 55.0 + 60.3 lg C
(Al3+) (R2 = 0.999), the limit of detection was calculated to be 1.3μmolL−1 (3σ, n = 9). The results illustrated this fluorescence enhancement method based on N-GQD could be used for determination of Al3+.

In order to apply the N-GQD sensor to detect Al3+ in practical sample, the interferences from other metal ions were estimated. The results (Fig. S4, Supplementary material) showed, in contrast with fluorescence enhancing signal deduced by Al3+, the interferences generated by Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ at 1 time concentration, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Co2+ at 10 times concentration, and Ba2+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Cl- at 1000 times concentration of Al3+ were all less than 5%, indicating the excellent selectivity for Al3+ detection with this method. This unique selectivity of N-GQD might be attributed to the smaller ionic radius and higher charge density of Al3+, which allowed suitable coordination geometry between the N-GQD and Al3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Al3+ in hydrotalcite tablets
ce:section.ce:para: In order to examine the applicability of the proposed method in real sample analysis, the content of Al3+ in the tablet solution was determined by this method and ICP-MS respectively. The content of the effective ingredient hydrotalcite (Al2Mg6(OH)16CO3·4H2O) in tablets could be calculated based on the concentration of Al3+, which was detected to be 49.3 ± 0.8% (w/w) with this method and 45.7 ± 3.4% by ICP-MS, the relative error was 7.9%. The results proved the fluorescence enhancement approach based on N-GQD could rapidly and accurately determine the concentration of Al3+ in practical samples.

The enhanced fluorescence of N-GQD by Al3+ might be quenched by strong metals chelator (such as EDTA). Therefore, the fluorescence switch property of N-GQD based on the control of Al3+ and EDTA was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5, the fluorescence of 16μgmL−1 N-GQD could dramatically increased by 50μmol L−1 Al3+, however, the enhanced fluorescence could be quenched by 80μmolL−1 EDTA due to the strong complexation between EDTA and Al3+ (lgK(EDTA-Al3+) = 16.1), the fluorescence of system was recovered again after adding Al3+ again. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQD was reversibly regulated by alternate addition of increasing Al3+concentrations and EDTA for four times this way, implying the relative excellent fluorescence switch character of N-GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Al3+ in HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells presented viability higher than 90% after incubation with N-GQD in the range of 0–400μgmL−1 (Fig. S5), which indicated the low toxicity of N-GQD to cells. The intake kinetics of N-GQD by HeLa cells was investigated through measuring the green fluorescence of N-GQD with confocal fluorescence microscopy. As shown in Fig. 6A, with increasing incubation time, the increasing amount of probe entered into cytoplasm and reached saturation after incubation for 3h. For subsequent Al3+ detection in HeLa cells through fluorescence increasing method, taking background signal and sensing sensitivity into account, the incubation time between HeLa cells and N-GQD was chosen as 2h.

Based on the N-GQD sensor exhibited different extent florescence increase responses to Al3+ in pH range of 3.0–8.0, the N-GQD sensor was used for detecting Al3+ in HeLa cells. The HeLa cells were incubated with N-GQD in an incubator for 2h. After washing with PBS, the cells were further incubated with different concentrations of Al3+ for 2h, and then washed with PBS. The contents of Al3+ in cells could be detected by confocal fluorescence microscopy. The images showed (Fig. 6B) the fluorescent signal in cells obviously enhanced after incubation with increasing concentrations of Al3+. To quantification Al3+ detection in cells with N-GQD, the above cells were harvested by digestion with trypsin, and then broken by ultrasonic wave. After that, the supernatant of cells were separated and measured by fluorescence spectrometer. As the results displayed (Fig. S6), compared with control cells (Fig S6A, a), an obvious fluorescent signal was detected after incubation with N-GQD (Fig S6A, b), and fluorescent signals gradually increased after incubation with increasing concentrations of Al3+ (Fig S6A, c-f). However, the shift of maximum emission wavelength of N-GQD from 515nm to 530nm was observed, which might be the result of proteins in cells affected fluorescence of N-GQD. Additionally, according to fluorescence intensity of cells (Fig. S6B) and the linear relationship between concentration of N-GQD and its fluorescence intensity in aqueous solution, the total concentration of N-GQD per total of cells was evaluated. By counting the number of cells, the mass and the concentration of N-GQD in single cell were calculated to be 3.5pgand 93.6 μg mL−1 after incubation with N-GQD (100μgmL−1) for 2h. The results implied that the N-GQD was cell membrane permeable and able to response to Al3+ in living cells.

The N-GQD was prepared by facile solvothermal treatment with GO using dimethylformamide, and it showed direct and selective florescence enhance response towards Al3+. The mechanism of fluorescence enhancement was investigated to be the restriction of photo-induced electron transfer process of N-GQD itself by complexing with Al3+. The method was successfully applied to the detect Al3+ in practical sample. The sensitive and fast florescence enhancing method for Al3+ detection avoided interference factors existing in indirect quantification methods. What more, the fluorescence switch property of N-GQD was verified by regulating with Al3+ and EDTA, and the probe was firstly used to detect Al3+ in living cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81471697, 81771878), the Key Technology R&D Program of Hubei Province (2014BBB003) and Yellow Crane Talent (Science & Technology) Program of Wuhan City and Applied Basic Research Program of Wuhan City (2016060101010044), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Hust, 2016YXMS253). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Center (HUST) for the help of measurement.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.08.057.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta Part A-Mol. BioMol. Spectrosc.
Mater. Sci. Eng. C-Mater. Biol. Appl.
